perFrameCode: // impuls transmission with an angular momentum factor, 3 bodies
// good luck with tuning these values to a fine dynamic behavior ;-)
bouncedampening = 0.94;  // This is where kinetic energy is taken from the system.
// Be careful with values close to 1.0
bounceimpact = 0.17;     // When an object hits one border, it slightly penetrates it.
// The impact value determines how strong this depth is taken as resulting impuls.
// This is where kinetic energy possibly is added to the system depent on the increase in size.
// bouncing off borders (the first rotating object)
y1  = y1+vy1; // adding velocity vector to position
vr1 = asin(vr/r); // angular velocity of the surface
// bouncing off borders (the second rotating object)
// bouncing off borders (the third rotating object)
bounce = below( sqrt( sqr(x1+vx1-x2-vx2) + sqr(y1+vy1-y2-vy2)), 2*r); // is distance < diameter ?
bounce = bounce*below(sqrt( sqr(x1+vx1-x2-vx2) + sqr(y1+vy1-y2-vy2)),sqrt( sqr(x1-x2) + sqr(y1-y2))); // detect only if objects
ref_ang = atan2(x2-x1,y2-y1)+asin(1); // angle of the common tangent
vr = sin(vr1)*r; // angular velocity of the surface
// in case of a bounce, the velocity is split into the horizontal and vertical part relative to the common tangent,
// then the vertical aspects get swapped.
// a bounce goes 90% into impuls transmission and 10% angular momentum transmission.
// these values make quite a plausible impression, but in fact this has pretty little to do with physical correctness ;)
perPixelCode: c = 42; // impacts the "sharpness" of the sigmoid filter function
d = q3*0.6; // radius of the sigmoid filter function
